aboutPage.heroBody: The designer who tells you to slow down.
aboutPage.whoIAm:
I'm Cherry — a Vietnamese designer studying Design and Visual Communications at SCAD, and I make things that ask people to slow down.

My recurring subject is **time and memory**: waiting for coffee to brew, revisiting an old reflection, reclaiming the minutes we spend standing in line. A lot of that comes from home — my grandpa waiting for his coffee on the porch in Vietnam, Sài Gòn coffee talk, the unhurried pace of Đà Nẵng. Where I come from shows up in what I make.

Before SCAD I was a yearbook design lead and did theatre — writing, stage design, acting — so I think like a storyteller first and build product second. I also draw characters (say hi to Wally), which keeps an illustrator's warmth in my work.

*Design with empathy* isn't a tagline I decorate with. It's the whole argument.

experience.desc: Hosted by Innovative Design @ USC, theme "Design Against Efficiency." Built Everloop with Aarya Mahapatra — memory architecture for keeping New Year's resolutions.
Designed UI components and shaped the visual identity for an app that turns in-between moments into meaningful actions.

Where most of the work on this site was born.

caseStudies.title: Aurora
caseStudies.oneliner: A jewelry brand where fashion, legacy, and technology meet.
caseStudies.pullQuote: A locket can be a music box of memory.

sections.headline: Jewelry that carries more than light
sections.body: Aurora is a jewelry brand built on the belief that fashion, legacy, and technology can meet in one object. The centerpiece is a locket — a *music box of memory* — a piece you wear because of what it holds, not just how it catches light.
I built the brand system end to end: brand message, tone of voice, logo and logo color, typography, 3D product renders, and the app interface that lives alongside the physical pieces.

sections.headline: Inspiring. Comforting. Futuristic.
sections.body: The voice balances three registers — inspiring, comforting, futuristic — held together by consistency, authenticity, and connection. Every slide, label, and screen had to sound like the same warm, forward-looking brand.

The identity pairs an elegant italic serif with lush coral-to-peach gradients and halftone floral texture, carried across the deck, the 3D renders of the locket and necklace, and the app screens.

*TODO(cherry): add deck frames, renders, and app screens.*

caseStudies.title: Everloop
caseStudies.oneliner: Turning past experiences into future motivation — Record, Reflect, Revisit.
caseStudies.award: 1st place — CreateSC 2026 UI/UX Designathon, hosted by Innovative Design @ USC

sections.headline: A 1st-place designathon project about long-term motivation
sections.body:
Everloop was created during CreateSC 2026, a UI/UX Designathon hosted by Innovative Design at USC. The theme was **Design Against Efficiency**, which pushed us to think beyond speed, optimization, and short-term engagement.

Instead of designing another fast productivity tool, we asked how past experiences could help people stay connected to future goals.

media.caption: Screens from the CreateSC 2026 presentation and interface direction.

sections.headline: Responding to design against efficiency
sections.body: New Year's resolutions often fail because the tools around them are designed for speed, streaks, and short-term optimization. We wanted to challenge that pattern by designing for continuity instead: how memory, reflection, and revisiting past effort can support motivation over time.

sections.headline: Memory architecture
sections.body: Our concept centered on **Memory Architecture**: the idea that content gains meaning over time. A note you write today is not only a record of progress; it can become encouragement later, when motivation is low and a reminder of why you started matters most.

sections.headline: Record, reflect, revisit
sections.body:
Everloop helps users stay consistent with New Year's resolutions through a continuous feedback loop built around three actions:

- **Record** experiences and progress
- **Reflect** on what was learned or achieved
- **Revisit** those moments when motivation is low

By connecting these actions, Everloop turns memory into a source of motivation rather than treating productivity as speed.

media.caption: The system focuses on an emotional loop rather than a fast task loop.

sections.headline: 1st place at CreateSC 2026
sections.body: Everloop placed **1st** at the CreateSC 2026 UI/UX Designathon hosted by Innovative Design at USC. The project became a milestone not only because of the result, but because it pushed me to think more deeply about behavior design, habit formation, and designing for long-term impact instead of short-term engagement.

sections.headline: Designing for impact over engagement
sections.body: This project clarified a direction I keep returning to: products can help people by slowing them down, helping them remember, and reconnecting them with their own progress. Everloop made that idea tangible.

caseStudies.title: Mugmood
caseStudies.oneliner: A journaling app that turns time waiting for coffee into a moment for reflection.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Whenever everything feels fast, choose slow.

sections.headline: A coffee-inspired concept about slowing down
sections.body: Mugmood started from a design prompt about slowing down. Instead of treating waiting as dead time, the concept asks what it would mean to turn a small daily pause into a ritual of reflection.

sections.headline: A porch in Vietnam
sections.body:
While brainstorming, I kept returning to a childhood memory: my grandpa sitting on the front porch, waiting for his coffee to brew. That moment felt peaceful, unhurried, and deeply human.

The project draws from Vietnamese coffee culture, especially the quiet rhythm of street coffee conversations in Sài Gòn and the slower everyday pace I associate with Đà Nẵng.

media.caption: Visual references from the LinkedIn handoff folder for Mugmood.

sections.headline: A ritual-based experience that helps users pause
sections.body: I wanted Mugmood to capture the feeling of choosing a slower moment when everything around you feels too fast. Instead of treating productivity as speed, the concept asks users to pause, sit with a feeling, and reconnect with themselves through a small ritual.

sections.headline: Warm, calm, coffee-inspired
sections.body: Mugmood became a visual and interaction exploration around mood, memory, and slowness. Warm coffee tones, soft iconography, and a gentle editorial rhythm support the idea that reflection can be comforting rather than performative.

media.caption: Mugmood visuals lean into warmth, mood, and quiet pacing.

sections.headline: Designing from memory
sections.body: This project reminded me that cultural memory can be a real design material. Mugmood is not just about coffee. It is about the kind of slowness that lives inside everyday rituals and the feeling of home those rituals can hold.

caseStudies.title: Tessera
caseStudies.oneliner: Turning fragmented in-between moments into simple, meaningful actions.
caseStudies.team: With Steven Yanzhi Chen, Anna Vorontsova, and Jiwan Park — Cornell UX Design Club (CUxD) hackathon
caseStudies.pullQuote: Use QuickStart to make the most of your small moments.

sections.headline: A calm productivity companion for in-between moments
sections.body:
Tessera began with a small observation: most of us reach for our phones during the tiny gaps in the day. Waiting for the bus, standing in line, or sitting between plans can quickly become another moment of automatic scrolling.

During a design hackathon with Cornell UX Design Club, our team explored how those small pockets of time could become more intentional.

sections.headline: Fragmented time becomes mindless scrolling
sections.body: Students and Gen Z users often default to mindless scrolling during fragmented moments because most digital tools are built either for full attention or endless distraction. We wanted to design for the in-between: small pockets of time that still deserve intention.

sections.headline: Simple, low-friction micro-actions
sections.body: We designed Tessera as a calm productivity companion for fragmented moments, helping users write reflections, set goals, create short to-do lists, and complete timed actions without getting pulled into distraction.

media.caption: LinkedIn images from the Cornell UX Design Club hackathon build.

sections.headline: UI components and visual identity
sections.body: My main contribution was designing UI components and helping shape the visual identity. I focused on making the product feel calm, low-friction, and visually coherent even within the pace of a hackathon.

sections.headline: Shared constraints, quick decisions, real friendships
sections.body: The project became memorable beyond the screen: cancelled food plans, one meal a day, and a very hungry team somehow turned into part of the story. More than just a hackathon project, Tessera became a reminder that design is often built through shared constraints, fast decisions, and the friendships formed while solving the problem together.

media.caption: Additional Tessera images from the handoff folder.

caseStudies.title: Wally
caseStudies.oneliner: A student wallet app — one place to learn, save, and manage money.
caseStudies.team: Aarya Mahapatra, Ashley Cho, Jiwan Park, Anna Vorontsova, and Pramit Ranjan

sections.headline: A first UX class project about student financial literacy
sections.body: Wally was my first major UX class project and a strong starting point for my design journey. Our team focused on a real student problem: financial literacy matters, but most college students do not have a simple place to learn money skills, save, and stay on top of everyday financial decisions.

sections.headline: Students need one place to learn, save, and manage money
sections.body: Financial literacy is essential for college students, but students often lack one clear place to learn, save, and manage their money habits. We wanted the app to feel educational without becoming intimidating.

sections.headline: A friendly student wallet app
sections.body: We designed Wally as a student wallet app that brings learning and money management into one approachable experience. The project helped me understand how research, interface structure, prototyping, storytelling, and character design all work together inside a product.

media.caption: Wally app and character materials from the LinkedIn handoff images.

sections.headline: Wally character design and visual direction
sections.body: My main contribution was creating the Wally character and supporting the team's visual direction. That work taught me how personality can make a functional product feel more approachable without losing clarity.

sections.headline: An early lesson in collaborative UX
sections.body:
I also learned very quickly how easy it is to get lost inside a Figma file when the system grows. That became part of the learning: staying organized, designing with the team in mind, and building something that felt friendly without losing function.

Looking back, Wally feels like a checkpoint from the beginning of my UX journey. It was a project about financial literacy, but it also taught me how much good design depends on collaboration, clarity, and showing up consistently with a team.

media.caption: Additional Wally process and presentation frames.
